seeds.direction: Human validation of LLM judge labels on stratified sample for RDI calibration
seeds.rationale: All reported Hal_TP, Hal_Gen, and RDI values flow through a single LLM judge (gemini-2.5-flash). The paper explicitly flags judge-dependence as a limitation. Validating against expert annotation would tighten RDI's cardinal interpretation and assess whether the directional ordering is robust to judge choice.
seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Single LLM judge for all metrics; RDI framed as directional signal precisely because of absence of human validation (C6, C7).
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The evaluation judge's judgments reflect true ground-truth equivalence. No human-annotated validation sample exists in the paper.
seeds.research_question: Does the LLM judge (gemini-2.5-flash) agree with expert legal annotators on clause equivalence judgments, and how does disagreement affect RDI values?
seeds.hypothesis: Expert annotators will show high agreement with the LLM judge on clear cases (exact numeric/temporal mismatches) but lower agreement on scope and obligation cases where legal interpretation matters, leading to systematic RDI shifts.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Sample 200 contradicted clauses stratified by mismatch_type (50 each from missing_condition, extra_condition, scope, numeric/temporal/obligation). Have 3 legal experts label each as correct/incorrect and provide mismatch_type. Compute Cohen's κ against LLM judge. Re-estimate RDI using expert labels only.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If expert-LLM agreement is high (κ > 0.8) across all types, the single-judge limitation is weak. If agreement is low on scope errors (the dominant category), RDI's directional interpretation may be unstable.
seeds.killer_objection: Expert annotation at scale is expensive and slow — the single-LLM judge approach trades rigor for practicality.
seeds.killer_result: Even moderate expert-judge disagreement (κ > 0.6) on a small stratified sample would indicate the directional ordering is robust enough for deployment decisions.
seeds.successor_paper_hint: Dahl et al. (2024) — legal hallucination typology could provide expert annotation methodology

seeds.direction: Cross-jurisdiction and multi-lingual generalization of the typed failure ordering
seeds.rationale: The paper only tests CUAD English US commercial contracts. Whether the typed gap (obligation/numeric ≫ temporal) transfers to other document types, jurisdictions (civil law, Sharia), or languages is unknown. The auditing method is claimed to transfer, but specific failure rates must be re-measured.
seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Limitations stated: results apply to CUAD-style English US commercial contracts; generalization across jurisdictions and document types remains to be verified (C18).
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The typed failure ordering is a property of LLMs and legal text structure, not of the English US contract genre specifically.
seeds.research_question: Does the within-model typed gap of ~40 pp (obligation/numeric ≫ factual ≳ temporal) replicate on non-US contracts (EU GDPR clauses, UK employment contracts, Indian commercial agreements) and non-English legal documents?
seeds.hypothesis: The typed ordering transfers because it reflects the inherent verification complexity of claim types (multi-element obligation vs. single-value temporal) rather than genre-specific patterns. However, absolute rates will shift due to different legal conventions.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Translate or construct a 200-contract benchmark across 3 jurisdictions (EU civil law, UK common law, Indian mixed system) with expert-annotated clause types mapped to the same 4 categories. Evaluate gemini-3-flash and qwen3-32b. Compute per-jurisdiction typed gaps.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If a jurisdiction shows reversed ordering (e.g., temporal failing more than obligation), the gap is not universal and may reflect US-specific drafting complexity.
seeds.killer_objection: Building cross-jurisdiction contract benchmarks requires extensive legal expertise and resource investment.
seeds.killer_result: If the typed gap is ≥25 pp in all three jurisdictions, the auditing method is broadly transferable.
seeds.successor_paper_hint: LegalBench (Guha et al., 2023) — includes some non-US legal tasks

seeds.rationale: The typed debate pipeline reduces fabrications by 45% but barely touches content contradictions (−0.2%). The paper acknowledges this is consistent with prior work (Huang et al., 2024) showing LLMs cannot self-correct reasoning. A crucial open problem is how to repair within-clause content errors.
seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Content contradictions moved only 642→641 (−0.2%) while fabrications dropped 524→287 (−45%). Skeptic and backbone share same parametric priors (C14).
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Current LLM debate mechanisms cannot reliably correct content errors in genuinely present clauses because debating agents share the same parametric knowledge base.
seeds.research_question: Can citation-grounded verification (retrieving and quoting the exact contract sentence) combined with asymmetric content-correction gates repair within-clause content errors in obligation and numeric claims?
seeds.hypothesis: Providing the Judge with the exact contract passage containing the disputed clause segment (rather than debating from parametric memory) will enable content correction on 15–25% of contradicted clauses, going beyond fabrication filtering.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Augment the typed debate pipeline with a Contract Retriever that, for each contradicted clause, retrieves the most relevant 3 sentences from the contract. Pass these to the Judge as additional evidence. Compare correction rates on 500 contradicted clauses from Experiment 2.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If citation grounding does not improve content correction, the bottleneck is not information access but the Judge's inability to correctly apply retrieved evidence — a more fundamental reasoning limitation.
seeds.killer_objection: Citation grounding adds retrieval latency and cost; for long contracts, precision of the retriever becomes a new failure mode.
seeds.killer_result: Even 10% content contradiction reduction would be significant given the 58.6% best-configuration contradiction rate.
seeds.successor_paper_hint: Huang et al. (2024) — self-correction limitations contextualize this challenge

seeds.direction: Ablation study: isolating the contribution of each debate pipeline component
seeds.rationale: The calibrated debate pipeline has multiple novel components: typed Skeptic challenges, re-extract node, asymmetric Add/Del gates, and the 6-role architecture. Which components drive the 2.8-point composite score improvement? Without ablations, the individual contribution of each design choice is unknown.
seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: The pipeline combines multiple novel design choices: typed Skeptic challenges, re-extract node, asymmetric gates, 6-role architecture. No ablation isolates their individual contributions (C9, C10, C16).
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Every component contributes meaningfully to the overall improvement. In reality, some may be redundant or even harmful.
seeds.research_question: What is the marginal contribution of (a) typed Skeptic challenges vs. generic Skeptic, (b) re-extract node, (c) asymmetric gates vs. symmetric gates, and (d) the Verifier role on gemma-4-26B-A4B debate performance?
seeds.hypothesis: Typed Skeptic challenges and asymmetric gates contribute the most; the re-extract node contributes little due to low structural error frequency; the Verifier has a small but non-zero effect through the addition gate check.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Run 5 ablation configurations on the same 120-contract subset: (1) no Skeptic (just Verifier+Judge), (2) generic Skeptic (non-typed challenges), (3) no re-extract node, (4) symmetric gates, (5) full pipeline (reproduce). Compare composite scores.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If generic Skeptic performs within 0.2 score points of typed Skeptic, the type-specific challenge formulation is not the driver — the gate asymmetry or pipeline structure may be more important.
seeds.killer_objection: 5 configurations × gemma-4-26B-A4B compute cost is substantial, especially for a single-run experiment.
seeds.killer_result: If typed Skeptic alone accounts for >1.5 score points of the 2.8-point improvement, the calibration claim is strongly supported.
seeds.successor_paper_hint: Du et al. (2024) — multi-agent debate provides baseline for generic Skeptic comparison

seeds.direction: Risk-aware model selection: operationalizing RDI-informed procurement
seeds.rationale: The paper shows RDI separates models with matched aggregate rates and argues for direction-aware procurement, but provides no framework for how compliance officers should weight RDI vs. other metrics in specific deployment contexts.
seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: RDI differentiates gpt-5.2 (+0.161) from qwen3-32b (−0.202) despite matched ~52% Hal_TP. Paper states 'no single model is universally correct' and the choice depends on task asymmetry (C6).
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Compliance officers can translate RDI into deployment decisions without a decision-theoretic framework connecting RDI → legal exposure.
seeds.research_question: Can a weighted multi-criteria decision model incorporating RDI, Hal_TP, FAR, and task-specific risk tolerances produce procurement recommendations that dominate aggregate-rate-based selection?
seeds.hypothesis: An RDI-weighted utility function where the sign and magnitude of the RDI penalty depends on deployment context (compliance vs. legal operations) will recommend different models than aggregate Hal_TP ranking, and these recommendations will align with domain experts' preferences.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Design 2 decision scenarios (high-compliance: regulatory filing review; high-throughput: contract abstraction for operations). Have 5 procurement officers rate 6 model configurations from Experiment 2. Compare aggregate-Hal_TP ranking vs. RDI-informed weighted ranking against expert preferences.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If experts rank models similarly to aggregate Hal_TP regardless of RDI, the practical value of directional signals for procurement may be limited.
seeds.killer_objection: This is a human-subjects study requiring access to legal procurement professionals; hard to conduct at scale.
seeds.killer_result: If RDI-informed ranking disagrees with aggregate-Hal_TP ranking in at least 60% of cases and aligns with expert preferences, the framework has clear practical utility.
seeds.successor_paper_hint: Dahl et al. (2024) — legal professional impact assessment methodology

seeds.direction: Expanding typed categories: subtype-level profiles within Obligation/Entitlement
seeds.rationale: The paper aggregates 27 obligation clause types into one category. Appendix E.5 reveals a 42.7–88.6% Hal_TP range across subtypes (within-bucket SD 12.4 pp). Some obligation subtypes perform near the temporal mean. Subtype-level profiling could enable more granular calibration.
seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Obligation subtype Hal_TP ranges from 42.7% (Termination for Convenience) to 88.6% (Post-Termination Services). Even the lowest lies above temporal mean, but the variation matters for deployment (App. E.5).
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: All 27 obligation clause types share similar failure characteristics because they share the 'obligation' structural category. In reality, failure rates vary 2× across subtypes.
seeds.research_question: Does subtype-level Skeptic challenge calibration (targeting Post-Termination Services vs. Termination for Convenience differently) outperform category-level calibration for the Obligation type?
seeds.hypothesis: Subtype-targeted Skeptic challenges — e.g., focusing on service-level specifications for Post-Termination Services — will reduce the 88.6% subtype hallucination rate more than generic 'modal verb and carve-out' challenges.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Select the 5 highest-failing obligation subtypes (Post-Termination Services, Affiliate License-Licensor, Source Code Escrow, IP Ownership Assignment, Revenue/Profit Sharing). Design subtype-specific Skeptic challenges. Apply debate pipeline on these 5 subtypes and measure per-subtype ΔFAR and ΔHal_Gen vs. generic obligation Skeptic.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If subtype-specific Skeptic challenges do not outperform generic obligation Skeptic, the gains from finer granularity are marginal and the category-level calibration is sufficient.
seeds.killer_objection: 27 subtype-specific Skeptic prompts increase pipeline complexity and may not scale across different contract corpora.
seeds.killer_result: Even 3 of 5 subtypes showing ≥3 additional pp FAR reduction would justify the granularity investment.
seeds.successor_paper_hint: CUAD (Hendrycks et al., 2021) — provides the 41 clause types with expert definitions

seeds.direction: Content-correction via cross-model jury with stronger backbones
seeds.rationale: The paper notes that the Skeptic and baseline extraction share the same parametric priors (gemma-4-26B-A4B), which limits content correction. A cross-model jury where Skeptic, Supporter, and Judge use different model backbones could break the shared-prior bottleneck.
seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Content contradictions unchanged because 'the baseline extraction and Skeptic share the same parametric priors' (C14). Shared priors limit the diversity of perspectives in debate.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Debate agents must share the same backbone for effective structured debate. This assumption may be violated productively.
seeds.research_question: Does cross-model debate (Skeptic on gemini-3-flash, Supporter on gemma-4-26B-A4B, Judge on gpt-5.2) improve content contradiction rates beyond same-backbone debate?
seeds.hypothesis: A Skeptic with different parametric priors can identify content errors that the extraction backbone's own priors would miss, enabling the Judge to correct 5–10% of contradicted clauses.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Run cross-model debate on 500 contradicted clauses from Experiment 2 using: Skeptic=gemini-3-flash, Supporter=gemma-4-26B-A4B (same as extraction backbone), Judge=gpt-5.2. Compare Hal_Gen and content correction rate vs. same-backbone debate (all gemma-4-26B-A4B).
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If cross-model debate does not improve content correction, the limitation is not shared priors but the fundamental difficulty of correcting generated text — consistent with Huang et al. (2024).
seeds.killer_objection: Cross-model debate multiplies inference costs by 3× (three different API backbones) — the cost advantage of the 4B-parameter backbone is lost.
seeds.killer_result: Even 5% content contradiction reduction in a cross-model setup would warrant investigation into cost-efficient cross-model designs.
seeds.successor_paper_hint: Huang et al. (2024) — LLMs cannot self-correct reasoning yet, contextualizing this approach

meta_analysis.most_impactful_single_experiment: Seed 1 (Human judge validation) — directly addresses the paper's most explicit limitation and would validate or challenge every reported metric.
meta_analysis.most_ambitious_direction: Seed 3 (Content-correction mechanisms) — addresses the fundamental limitation of the current pipeline but requires architectural innovation beyond the paper's scope.
meta_analysis.most_practical_direction: Seed 5 (RDI-informed procurement) — directly applies the paper's framework to real-world decision-making with a minimum viable experiment that is well-scoped.
meta_analysis.cross_seed_dependencies: Seed 1 (human validation) should precede Seed 5 (RDI procurement) because procurement recommendations depend on RDI reliability. Seed 4 (ablation) should precede Seed 3 (content correction) to isolate which components work before adding new ones.
